concept_name: Potential of a Point Charge — V = kQ/r (falls off as 1/r)
source_book: NCERT Class 12 Physics, Ch.2 Electrostatic Potential and Capacitance (§2.2 Potential due to a point charge) — table-of-contents + Indian-anchor reference only; teaching authored from first principles. HC Verma consulted for SEQUENCE (recall the equipotential shells already met in the prerequisite electric_potential_meaning → ask the unanswered question: how BIG is the potential V at a given distance r? → state the answer V = kQ/r, point P's shell lights up with its value → read the formula: r sits downstairs to the FIRST power, one power of r, not r squared → confront the 1/r-squared instinct: halve r and the field-trained student predicts V quadruples, but V only DOUBLES because V falls as 1/r → put both falloffs on one graph: the bright V = kQ/r (1/r) curve rides ABOVE the dimmed E = kQ/r² (1/r²) ghost far out → V is a SIGNED SCALAR: flip Q's sign and the V number flips sign, hill becomes well, no arrow ever → hand the explorer the slider: drag r, watch V climb gently on the 1/r curve), never copied. The atomic idea: the electric potential of a point charge at distance r is V = kQ/r — it falls off as 1/r, ONE power of r, which is SLOWER than the field's 1/r²; so halving r only DOUBLES V (not quadruples it), and V reaches farther out than the force you would feel. V is a signed scalar (no arrow): for +Q it is a positive hill, for −Q a negative well, with V(∞)=0. SCOPE: this is the point-charge FORMULA/VALUE diamond of Ch.2 — it teaches V = kQ/r and its 1/r falloff. It declares electric_potential_meaning (what V MEANS, V = W/q) as a prerequisite and does NOT re-teach it. It does NOT cover superposition of potentials from MULTIPLE charges, the gradient relation E = −dV/dr, the potential of a dipole, capacitance, or conductor equipotential. The headline contrast is V ∝ 1/r vs E ∝ 1/r² — voltage reaches farther than force. NEW field_3d potential primitives reuse the point_charge_positive scenario and the electric_potential_meaning shell/explorer primitives (bunched-near-the-charge V-labelled equipotential shells with per-state reveal, a halve-r predict→reveal beat with a live V count-up, a V-vs-r curve panel with a BRIGHT 1/r curve over a DIMMED 1/r² ghost meeting at r0=2, a sign-flip recolor, and a draggable potential_explorer with a live curve dot). Prerequisites electric_potential_meaning + electric_field_point_charge + coulombs_law. Conceptual-only (no mode_overrides), EPIC-L-first (no epic_c_branches; the three misconceptions are confronted inside EPIC-L via Rule 16a misconception_watch + predict→reveal).

real_world_anchor.primary: In a school physics lab, the metal dome of a Van de Graaff generator is charged to a high voltage. Stand near it and the voltage you would measure stays high even a fair way out — it drops only GENTLY with distance. But the force you would actually feel, the push on a small charge, dies away fast as you step back. That gap is the whole idea: the potential V of the dome falls off as one over the distance r, while the force, the field E, falls off as one over r squared. Voltage reaches farther than force.
real_world_anchor.secondary: Run a plastic comb through dry hair and hold it near a thin stream of water. Close to the comb the pull on the water is strong; step the comb back and the pull weakens quickly — that is the field's one-over-r-squared. But the potential the comb sets up in the air around it fades more slowly, one-over-r, lingering farther out than the force does.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: A charged thundercloud sits at a huge potential relative to the ground far below. The potential reaches all the way down to the ground — one-over-r is a long, gentle reach — even though the actual force on a stray charge high in the air, the field, has fallen off much faster, as one-over-r-squared.

Q.name: Source charge on the dome (kept at +1 across the falloff arc; its SIGN flips in the sign-flip states)

r.name: Distance of the field point P from the source charge (the STATE_6 drag owns this; drives which shell P sits on and the live V readout)

k.name: Coulomb constant k = 1/(4*pi*epsilon_0) (the k in V = k*Q/r)

epsilon_0.name: Permittivity of free space (a fixed constant of nature)

DEMO_V_PER_NC.name: Demo potential readout per demo-nC of source charge at unit demo radius — THE round number the whole 1/r series rides on (12 gives the shell table V=24/12/8/6/4/3 at r=0.5/1/1.5/2/3/4 and STATE_3's exact 6->12 doubling)
DEMO_V_PER_NC.unit: demo V per (demo nC) at r=1

charge_sign.name: Sign of the source charge (+1 hill, -1 well) — flips V's sign without ever adding an arrow

formulas.scalar_sign: V is a signed scalar; +Q is a hill (V>0), -Q a well (V<0), no arrow

constraints: The potential of a point charge falls off as 1/r, while its field falls off as 1/r^2: V ~ 1/r is ONE power of r gentler than E ~ 1/r^2 (C1).

Halving the distance r only DOUBLES the potential V (it does not quadruple it): V = k*Q/r, so r -> r/2 gives V -> 2V, NOT 4V - the 4x quadrupling is the field's 1/r^2 behaviour, not the potential's (C2).
The potential V is a signed SCALAR: one number per point, no direction and no arrow - flipping the sign of the source charge flips the SIGN of V (hill to well), never adds a direction (C3).
The reference is V(infinity) = 0 and V = k*Q/r is finite for every r >= 0.05 (the demo clamps r at 0.05 to avoid the unphysical r=0 blow-up) (C4).
The Coulomb constant is k = 8.99e9 N*m^2/C^2; the on-screen V numbers use round DEMO units (DEMO_V_PER_NC = 12) so the 1/r series reads 24/12/8/6/4/3, not SI volts (C5).
Far from the charge V rides ABOVE E: because V fades as 1/r and E dies as 1/r^2, the two meet at r0 = 2 and then diverge, V staying larger - voltage reaches farther than force (C6).

aha_moment.statement: V = kQ/r fades as 1/r, gentler than E's 1/r² — V rides above E.
aha_moment.visual_confirmation: On one V-vs-r graph, the bright V = kQ/r curve (1/r) and the dimmed E = kQ/r² ghost (1/r²) meet at r = 2, then split apart with the bright V curve riding visibly above the dim E ghost as r grows.

STATE_1.title: Back at the same +Q dome — how big is V at distance r?

scene_composition.text: shells of equal potential V (you know these)
each shell has one V number — its size at distance r comes next

tts_sentences.text_en: We are back at the same charged dome, like a school Van de Graaff generator. You already know the shells of equal potential V wrap around it.
tts_sentences.text_hi: हम उसी charged dome पर वापस आ गए हैं, जैसे एक school Van de Graaff generator होता है। आपको पहले से ही पता है कि equal potential V के shells इसे wrap करते हैं।
tts_sentences.text_te: మనం అదే charged dome వద్దకు తిరిగి వచ్చాము, ఇది school Van de Graaff generator లాంటిది. equal potential V యొక్క shells దాని చుట్టూ wrap అవుతాయని మీకు ఇప్పటికే తెలుసు.

tts_sentences.text_en: Each shell carries one number, the potential V on that shell. What we have not yet named is how BIG that number is at a given distance r.
tts_sentences.text_hi: हर shell एक number कैरी करता है, उस shell पर potential V। अब तक हमने यह नहीं बताया कि एक given distance r पर वह number कितना BIG होता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: ప్రతి shell ఒక number ని కలిగి ఉంటుంది, ఆ shell పై potential V. ఒక given distance r వద్ద ఆ number ఎంత BIG గా ఉంటుందో మనం ఇంకా చెప్పలేదు.

misconception_watch.belief: Potential is just a vague 'amount of charge' on the dome, not a definite number you can read off at a chosen distance.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: The faint equipotential shells each carry one plain V number, and the hook asks for the value at a specific distance r - a single definite number is waiting to be named.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: At every distance r the potential has one exact value, V = kQ/r; this diamond names that number.

STATE_2.title: The answer: V = kQ/r — one power of r, not r squared

scene_composition.text: field point P at distance r
shell at P lights up: V = 8
V = k Q / r   (r downstairs, one power)

tts_sentences.text_en: Pick a point P out here at distance r from the charge Q. The task is to find the potential V exactly there.
tts_sentences.text_hi: यहाँ charge Q से distance r पर एक point P चुनिए। हमारा task है वहाँ का potential V exactly निकालना।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఇక్కడ charge Q నుండి distance r వద్ద ఒక point P ని ఎంచుకోండి. అక్కడ potential V ని exactly కనుగొనడమే మన task.

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is the answer. The potential V = k Q / r. The shell at P lights up and shows its value.
tts_sentences.text_hi: यहाँ answer है। potential V = k Q / r। P पर shell light up होता है और अपनी value दिखाता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఇక్కడ answer ఉంది. potential V = k Q / r. P వద్ద ఉన్న shell light up అవుతుంది మరియు దాని value ను చూపుతుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: Read the formula carefully: the distance r is downstairs, to the first power. One power of r, not r squared. Hold on to that.
tts_sentences.text_hi: formula को ध्यान से पढ़िए: distance r downstairs है, to the first power। r की one power, r squared नहीं। इसे याद रखिए।
tts_sentences.text_te: formula ను జాగ్రత్తగా చదవండి: distance r downstairs ఉంది, to the first power. r యొక్క one power, r squared కాదు. దీన్ని గుర్తుంచుకోండి.

misconception_watch.belief: The potential of a point charge must fall off as 1/r squared, just like the field does.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: The formula callout writes V = kQ/r with r downstairs to the FIRST power only, and the shell at P shows its single value - one power of r, planted before the misconception is tested.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: Potential has only ONE power of r downstairs: V = kQ/r, gentler than the field's 1/r squared.

STATE_3.title: Halve r — the 1/r² instinct says ×4, but V only DOUBLES

scene_composition.text: halve r: from r = 2 in to r = 1

tts_sentences.text_en: Point P sits at distance r = 2, where the potential V reads 6. Now I halve the distance, bringing P in to r = 1.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Point P distance r = 2 पर है, जहाँ potential V 6 read करता है। अब मैं distance को halve करता हूँ, P को r = 1 पर लाता हूँ।
tts_sentences.text_te: Point P, distance r = 2 వద్ద ఉంది, అక్కడ potential V 6 అని చదువుతాము. ఇప్పుడు నేను distance ను halve చేస్తున్నాను, P ని r = 1 వద్దకు తీసుకువస్తున్నాను.

tts_sentences.text_en: The electric field E falls as one over r squared, so halving the distance r makes it four times bigger. The natural guess is that the potential V also quadruples, to 24.
tts_sentences.text_hi: electric field E, one over r squared के रूप में गिरता है, तो distance r को halve करने से यह four times bigger हो जाता है। natural guess यह है कि potential V भी four times बढ़कर 24 हो जाता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: electric field E, one over r squared గా తగ్గుతుంది, కాబట్టి distance r ని halve చేస్తే అది four times bigger అవుతుంది. natural guess ఏమిటంటే potential V కూడా four times పెరిగి 24 అవుతుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: Watch. The potential V only DOUBLES, from 6 to 12. Not four times — just twice.
tts_sentences.text_hi: देखिए। potential V केवल DOUBLES होता है, 6 से 12 तक। four times नहीं — बस twice।
tts_sentences.text_te: చూడండి. potential V కేవలం DOUBLES అవుతుంది, 6 నుండి 12 వరకు. four times కాదు — కేవలం twice.

tts_sentences.text_en: That is because the potential V = k Q / r falls as one over r, one power gentler than the field's one over r squared.
tts_sentences.text_hi: ऐसा इसलिए है क्योंकि potential V = k Q / r, one over r के रूप में fall होता है, जो field के one over r squared से one power gentler है।
tts_sentences.text_te: అది ఎందుకంటే potential V = k Q / r, one over r గా fall అవుతుంది, ఇది field యొక్క one over r squared కంటే one power gentler.

misconception_watch.belief: Halving the distance to a point charge makes the potential V four times bigger, because the student has internalised the field's 1/r squared falloff.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: P is pulled from r = 2 to r = 1 and the live V count climbs 6 -> 12, a clean DOUBLING, never reaching the predicted 24; the field's 4x is explicitly the wrong instinct.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: V = kQ/r falls as 1/r, so halving r DOUBLES V (6 -> 12); the 4x quadrupling belongs to the field's 1/r squared, not the potential.

STATE_4.title: Both falloffs on one graph: V (1/r) rides above E (1/r²)

scene_composition.text: dimmed ghost: E = k Q / r²  (steep 1/r²)
meet at r = 2, then split — V rides above E far out

tts_sentences.text_en: Let us see both falloffs on one graph: potential V up the side, distance r along the bottom.
tts_sentences.text_hi: आइए दोनों falloffs को एक graph पर देखते हैं: potential V side में ऊपर, distance r bottom के साथ।
tts_sentences.text_te: రెండు falloffs ని ఒక graph లో చూద్దాం: potential V పక్కన పైకి, distance r క్రింద along.

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is the potential V = k Q / r, a gentle one-over-r curve. Bright.
tts_sentences.text_hi: यहाँ potential V = k Q / r है, एक gentle one-over-r curve। Bright।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఇక్కడ potential V = k Q / r ఉంది, ఒక gentle one-over-r curve. Bright.

tts_sentences.text_en: Now fade in the electric field E = k Q / r squared beneath it, dimmed. The two meet at distance r = 2.
tts_sentences.text_hi: अब इसके नीचे electric field E = k Q / r squared fade in करें, dimmed। ये दोनों distance r = 2 पर मिलते हैं।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఇప్పుడు దీని క్రింద electric field E = k Q / r squared fade in చేయండి, dimmed. ఈ రెండూ distance r = 2 వద్ద కలుస్తాయి.

tts_sentences.text_en: Move outward and they split apart. The potential V rides ABOVE the electric field E far out — it fades slowly while the field dies fast. That gap is the whole idea: one power of r gentler.
tts_sentences.text_hi: बाहर की ओर जाएँ और वे अलग हो जाते हैं। Potential V, electric field E से बहुत दूर ABOVE रहता है — यह धीरे-धीरे fade होता है जबकि field तेज़ी से ख़त्म हो जाता है। वह gap ही पूरा idea है: one power of r gentler।
tts_sentences.text_te: బయటికి కదలండి మరియు అవి విడిపోతాయి. Potential V, electric field E కంటే చాలా దూరంగా ABOVE ఉంటుంది — అది నెమ్మదిగా fade అవుతుంది, అయితే field వేగంగా తగ్గిపోతుంది. ఆ gap నే మొత్తం idea: one power of r gentler.

misconception_watch.belief: The potential curve and the field curve are basically the same shape; kQ/r and kQ/r squared blur together.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: Two distinct curves on one graph - the bright 1/r potential visibly fades slower and rides ABOVE the dimmed 1/r squared field ghost for every r past their r = 2 crossing.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: kQ/r (potential) and kQ/r squared (field) are different curves: the 1/r potential is gentler and stays larger far out.

STATE_5.title: V is a SIGNED scalar: flip Q and the hill becomes a well — no arrow

scene_composition.text: V = k Q / r is a SCALAR — a signed number, no arrow
flip Q to −Q: the natural guess is an arrow appears
the V number flips to minus — a well, no arrow

tts_sentences.text_en: One more thing about the potential V = k Q / r. It is a SCALAR — a single signed number, no arrow, unlike the electric field E.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Potential V = k Q / r के बारे में एक और बात। यह एक SCALAR है — एक single signed number, कोई arrow नहीं, electric field E के विपरीत।
tts_sentences.text_te: Potential V = k Q / r గురించి ఇంకొక విషయం. ఇది ఒక SCALAR — ఒక single signed number, arrow ఉండదు, electric field E లా కాకుండా.

tts_sentences.text_en: For a positive charge Q the potential V is positive everywhere: a hill. Now I flip the charge Q to negative — the natural guess is that some arrow should appear.
tts_sentences.text_hi: एक positive charge Q के लिए potential V हर जगह positive होता है: एक hill। अब मैं charge Q को negative करता हूँ — natural guess यह है कि कोई arrow दिखना चाहिए।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఒక positive charge Q కోసం potential V ప్రతిచోటా positive గా ఉంటుంది: ఒక hill. ఇప్పుడు నేను charge Q ని negative కు flip చేస్తున్నాను — natural guess ఏమిటంటే ఏదో arrow కనిపించాలి.

tts_sentences.text_en: Watch. Flip the sign of the charge Q, and the potential V flips from plus to minus — a well, not a hill. No arrow ever appears. The sign is in the number, never a direction.
tts_sentences.text_hi: देखिए। Charge Q का sign flip करें, और potential V plus से minus हो जाता है — एक well, hill नहीं। कोई arrow कभी नहीं दिखता। Sign number में होता है, कभी direction में नहीं।
tts_sentences.text_te: చూడండి. Charge Q యొక్క sign ని flip చేయండి, మరియు potential V plus నుండి minus కు flip అవుతుంది — ఒక well, hill కాదు. ఎప్పుడూ arrow కనిపించదు. Sign number లో ఉంటుంది, ఎప్పుడూ direction లో ఉండదు.

misconception_watch.belief: The potential is a vector that points outward from a positive charge, so flipping the charge sign should make an arrow appear or reverse.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: Flipping Q to negative recolors the shells and flips every V label from plus to minus - a well instead of a hill - and NO arrow is ever drawn on the potential.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: V = kQ/r is a signed scalar: flipping the source charge flips V's SIGN (hill to well), it never adds or reverses a direction.

STATE_6.title: Explore: drag r, watch V climb gently on the 1/r curve

scene_composition.text: drag r — P's shell lights up, V reads out live
dot tracks the 1/r curve — V climbs GENTLY
flip Q to −Q: same gentle 1/r, now below zero

tts_sentences.text_en: Now you drive. Drag the field point in and out to change the distance r. The shell it lands on lights up and the potential V reads out live.
tts_sentences.text_hi: अब आप drive करें। Distance r बदलने के लिए field point को अंदर और बाहर drag करें। जिस shell पर यह गिरता है वह light up होता है और potential V live पढ़ता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఇప్పుడు మీరు drive చేయండి. Distance r ని మార్చడానికి field point ని లోపలికి మరియు బయటికి drag చేయండి. అది పడే shell light up అవుతుంది మరియు potential V live గా చదువుతుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: Watch the dot on the one-over-r curve track with you, and feel the potential V rise GENTLY as you pull the distance r in — doubling the closeness only doubles the potential V, never four times.
tts_sentences.text_hi: One-over-r curve पर dot को अपने साथ track करते हुए देखें, और potential V को GENTLY ऊपर उठते हुए महसूस करें जब आप distance r को अंदर खींचते हैं — closeness को doubling करने से केवल potential V doubles होता है, कभी four times नहीं।
tts_sentences.text_te: One-over-r curve పై ఉన్న dot మీతో పాటు track అవ్వడాన్ని చూడండి, మరియు మీరు distance r ని లోపలికి లాగినప్పుడు potential V GENTLY పెరుగుతుందని అనుభూతి చెందండి — closeness ని doubling చేయడం వల్ల potential V మాత్రమే doubles అవుతుంది, ఎప్పుడూ four times కాదు.

tts_sentences.text_en: Flip the charge Q to negative if you like, and the whole potential V hill becomes a well — same gentle one-over-r shape, just below zero.
tts_sentences.text_hi: अगर आप चाहें तो charge Q को negative करें, और पूरा potential V hill एक well बन जाता है — same gentle one-over-r shape, बस zero से नीचे।
tts_sentences.text_te: మీకు నచ్చితే charge Q ని negative కు flip చేయండి, మరియు మొత్తం potential V hill ఒక well అవుతుంది — అదే gentle one-over-r shape, కేవలం zero కంటే దిగువన.

assessment.mastery_definition: A student has mastered the potential of a point charge when they can (1) state V = kQ/r, the potential at distance r from a point charge Q; (2) recognise that V falls off as 1/r, ONE power of r, slower than the field's 1/r squared; (3) compute that halving r DOUBLES V (not quadruples it), distinguishing the potential's 1/r from the field's 1/r squared; (4) treat V as a signed scalar with no direction, so flipping the source charge's sign flips V's sign (hill to well) without adding an arrow; (5) explain why V is still appreciable where the force is nearly dead - because 1/r reaches farther than 1/r squared; and (6) plug numbers into V = kQ/r correctly, including reading the 1/r (not 1/r squared) dependence on r.

questions.stem: What is the electric potential V at a distance r from a point charge Q (with V = 0 at infinity)?

options.A: V = kQ/r - the charge over the distance, to the first power
options.B: V = kQ/r² - the charge over the distance squared
options.C: V = kQr - the charge times the distance
options.D: V = kQ/r³ - the charge over the distance cubed

distractor_misconceptions.B: uses the field's 1/r squared falloff for the potential, the central misconception this diamond confronts
distractor_misconceptions.C: makes V grow with distance instead of falling off
distractor_misconceptions.D: uses the dipole-potential 1/r squared or an invented 1/r cubed dependence

questions.tested_idea: The potential of a point charge is V = kQ/r, with a single power of r downstairs.
questions.parallel_form_stem: A small charged sphere acts as a point charge Q. Taking the potential to be zero infinitely far away, what is the potential V at distance r from it?

questions.stem: At a point a distance r from a point charge the potential is V = 6 (demo units). You move to half that distance, r/2. What is the potential there?

options.A: 12 - the potential doubles, because V = kQ/r falls as 1/r
options.B: 24 - the potential quadruples, because halving r multiplies it by four
options.C: 6 - the potential is unchanged, since it does not depend on distance
options.D: 3 - the potential halves when you move closer

distractor_misconceptions.B: applies the field's 1/r squared (x4 on halving r) to the potential - the key misconception
distractor_misconceptions.C: thinks potential is the same everywhere around the charge, ignoring the 1/r dependence
distractor_misconceptions.D: gets the direction of change backwards, lowering V on moving closer

questions.tested_idea: Halving r doubles V because V = kQ/r falls off as 1/r, not 1/r squared.
questions.parallel_form_stem: Near a charged dome the potential at some spot is V. You walk in to half the distance from the dome. By what factor does the potential change?

questions.stem: How do the potential V and the field E of a point charge fall off with distance r, and which one fades more slowly?

options.A: V ~ 1/r and E ~ 1/r²; V fades more slowly and stays larger far out
options.B: Both V and E fall off as 1/r², at the same rate
options.C: V ~ 1/r² and E ~ 1/r; the field fades more slowly
options.D: Both fall off as 1/r, so they stay equal everywhere

distractor_misconceptions.B: gives the potential the field's 1/r squared falloff, the core blur this diamond fixes
distractor_misconceptions.C: swaps the two falloffs, attaching 1/r squared to V and 1/r to E
distractor_misconceptions.D: makes both fall off as 1/r, erasing the one-power-of-r difference

questions.tested_idea: V ~ 1/r falls off one power of r more gently than E ~ 1/r squared, so V rides above E far from the charge.
questions.parallel_form_stem: Compare how the potential and the electric field of a point charge weaken as you move away. Which fades faster, and what is each one's power of r?

questions.stem: The source charge is changed from +Q to −Q. What happens to the potential V at a fixed point near it?

options.A: V flips sign, from positive to negative - a well instead of a hill, still a number with no direction
options.B: An arrow appears on V, pointing inward toward the negative charge
options.C: V keeps the same positive value, since potential has no sign
options.D: V becomes a vector pointing away from the charge

distractor_misconceptions.B: treats the scalar potential as a vector that gains a direction when the charge sign flips
distractor_misconceptions.C: forgets that V = kQ/r carries the sign of Q, so −Q makes V negative
distractor_misconceptions.D: conflates the scalar V with the vector field E, giving it a direction

questions.tested_idea: V is a signed scalar: flipping the source charge's sign flips V's sign (hill to well) without adding any direction.
questions.parallel_form_stem: Replace a positive point charge with an equal negative one. Describe what changes about the potential at a nearby fixed point.

questions.stem: Far from a point charge, the force you would feel on a test charge is almost nothing, yet the potential V is still clearly non-zero. Why?

options.A: Because V ~ 1/r fades more slowly than the force's E ~ 1/r², so V is still appreciable where E has nearly died
options.B: Because the potential and the force fall off at exactly the same rate, so this cannot happen
options.C: Because the potential is created by a battery, not by the charge
options.D: Because V actually grows with distance, so it is largest far away

distractor_misconceptions.B: assumes V and E share the same falloff, contradicting 1/r vs 1/r squared
distractor_misconceptions.C: invents an external source for the potential instead of the charge itself
distractor_misconceptions.D: makes V increase with distance, reversing the 1/r falloff

questions.tested_idea: V ~ 1/r reaches farther than the force's 1/r squared, so the potential is still appreciable where the field is nearly dead.
questions.parallel_form_stem: Step well back from a charged dome until the push on a small charge is barely detectable. Why is the voltage at that spot still clearly non-zero?

questions.stem: A point charge gives potential V = 12 (demo units) at distance r = 1. What is the potential at r = 3?

options.A: 4 - V = kQ/r so V scales as 1/r: tripling r divides V by 3
options.B: 1.33 - V scales as 1/r², so tripling r divides V by 9
options.C: 36 - V grows with distance, so tripling r triples V
options.D: 12 - V is the same at every distance

distractor_misconceptions.B: uses the field's 1/r squared (divide by 9) instead of the potential's 1/r (divide by 3)
distractor_misconceptions.C: makes V grow with r instead of falling off
distractor_misconceptions.D: treats V as independent of distance

questions.tested_idea: V = kQ/r scales as 1/r, so tripling the distance divides the potential by 3, not by 9.
questions.parallel_form_stem: At distance r = 1 from a point charge the potential is 12 demo units. Read off the potential at r = 4 using V = kQ/r.

STATE_1.label: Recall hook - same +Q dome, faint known shells

STATE_2.label: Answer V = kQ/r - shell at P lights up

STATE_3.label: Halve r - instinct says x4, V only x2 (6 to 12)

STATE_4.label: PRIMARY aha - V (1/r) curve over E (1/r2) ghost, meet at r=2

STATE_5.label: Sign flip - +Q hill to -Q well, no arrow

STATE_6.label: Explore - drag r, live V on the 1/r curve, sign toggle
